title: Recasing a damaged book
subtitle: Removing a worn case and fitting a new one to a sound text block
excerpt: A book whose case has split, detached, or deteriorated beyond repair can often be saved by separating the text block and housing it in a new case. Recasing is one of the most practical book-repair skills, applicable to most 20th-century case-bound books.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapters on repairs and restoration. The text block separation and recasing method described follows the convention documented in Cockerell and expanded in early-20th-century conservation manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: The sewn or adhesive-bound body of a book's pages, separate from the case (the hard cover).
The hard cover assembly of a book: two boards joined by a spine, covered in cloth, leather, or paper, and attached to the text block by gluing the endpapers.
An open-weave cloth glued across the spine of a sewn text block to reinforce the joints before the case is attached.

content.text: Most case-bound books fail at the joint, not at the sewing. The
splits at the hinge, or the endpaper pulls away from the board, while the
remains perfectly sound. In those cases the repair is a recase: separate the text block from the damaged cover, repair the block's spine if needed, make a new case, and attach it.

attrs.heading: Before starting
attrs.body: Assess whether the text block is worth recasing. If the sewing is broken, pages are loose, or the spine is crumbling, a fuller repair is needed before a new case goes on.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: For attaching the new mull and gluing endpapers into the new case.

items.name: Wheat-paste (cooked)
items.notes: Mixed with PVA to slow the tack when gluing the endpapers.

items.name: Book mull (super)
items.notes: A short strip, the width of the spine, to reinforce the spine if the original mull is damaged.

items.name: Washi kozo
items.notes: For lining or stabilising a fragile spine before mull is applied.

items.name: Craft knife
items.notes: For separating the endpapers from the boards.

items.name: Bookbinding board, 2 mm
items.notes: For the new case boards, cut to the text-block dimensions.

content.text: Separate the text block from the case

Open the book to the front endpaper. Work a bone folder gently along the joint between the pasted-down endpaper and the board, lifting the paper from the board rather than tearing it. Use a craft knife blade flat to the board surface where the paste refuses to release. Work slowly: the goal is to leave the endpaper attached to the text block. Once both endpapers are free, the block lifts away from the case.
Inspect and repair the text block spine
Remove any old adhesive from the spine by dampening gently with a soft brush of warm water and peeling the old lining away. Do not soak; the sewing thread must remain sound. Check the

wings: if they are still intact and well-adhered, leave them. If they have torn away, cut a new strip of book mull slightly shorter than the spine height and glue it across the spine with thinned PVA. Allow the spine to dry completely before proceeding.

Cut two fresh boards to match the original board dimensions exactly. The new case spine width equals the thickness of the text block plus 2 mm for ease. Cover the case in cloth or paper following the full-cloth or paper-covered case method, with the hinge groove set 4 mm from each spine edge. Allow the case to dry fully before casing in.

Brush a mixture of PVA and paste onto the exposed surface of the front endpaper, working from the centre outward. Open the new case and place the text block in position, centred between the boards with even squares at head, tail, and foredge. Press the board firmly onto the pasted endpaper. Close the book and work a bone folder along each hinge groove from outside. Repeat for the back endpaper. Press under boards and weight overnight.

A recased book is as strong as the original sewing allows. Store upright with enough neighbours to support the boards. If the text block is old or fragile, keep the book away from high humidity, which re-activates old adhesives and encourages mould.

items.cause: The original paste was applied heavily, or the paper is fragile.
items.fix: Stop and dampen the endpaper with a very slightly moist brush to reactivate the paste. Work slowly. If the paper tears through, repair with Japanese tissue before recasing.

items.symptom: The text block sits off-centre in the new case
items.cause: The spine width was not measured accurately, or the block was placed before the adhesive had time to tack.
items.fix: Let the adhesive rest 30 seconds before placing the block. The slight tack gives time to adjust before pressing.

items.symptom: The hinges feel stiff after casing in
items.cause: The hinge groove was not pressed firmly enough, or the PVA dried before the groove was formed.
items.fix: While the book is still slightly damp, open each board to 90 degrees and work the bone folder into the hinge repeatedly to soften it.
